item.title: Beyond Face and Gesture: Brain–Body–Social Dynamics in Human Interaction
item.description:
Human social interaction emerges not only through facial expressions and body gestures, but also through dynamic coupling among the brain, body, and social environment. While recent advances in affective computing and social signal processing have enabled increasingly accurate recognition of observable behaviors, understanding internal states such as stress and emotion requires going beyond surface-level analysis.

In this talk, I will present our recent studies on parent–child interaction that combine neural and physiological sensing with subjective assessments. I will discuss how neural and physiological activities are dynamically coupled through social interactions and how these dynamics relate to subjective feelings of stress. Finally, I will argue for a new direction in neuro-inspired AI that estimates and models the embodied nature of human emotional experiences beyond the static assessment of facial and gesture signals.

item.title: Face, Body, Interaction: The Future of Digital Humans?
item.description: Do we still need explicit representations for faces and bodies? As diffusion and vision-language models generate ever more convincing video of people, the temptation is to declare the parameterization era over. Pixels are the representation we have the most data for, but not necessarily the right abstraction for real-time, interactive, and controllable characters. In this keynote I will argue that interaction — not fidelity — is the key requirement for impactful digital human applications, and explore what that implies for implicit vs. explicit approaches.

item.title: Scaling 3D Digital Humans
item.description: In this talk, we explore the paradigm shift of bringing 3D digital human modeling into the era of large-scale foundation models. While 2D generative models have seen explosive growth, 3D human synthesis has often remained constrained by data scarcity and computational overhead. We bridge this gap by investigating the pre-training and post-training regimes for 3D avatars. We present findings demonstrating that pre-training on massive, diverse datasets—followed by targeted fine-tuning on high-fidelity, curated “clean” data—unlocks unprecedented generalization. This approach enables robust inference on out-of-distribution data, far surpassing traditional methods.

item.title: Training for Associate Editors, Reviewers, and Authors
item.description:
This presentation outlines best practices for associate editors, reviewers, and authors involved in the scholarly publication pipeline. It provides practical guidance to support each stage of manuscript handling and evaluation. The presentation will also address several sensitive but crucial topics, including effective review management, sound and transparent decision‑making, plagiarism prevention, the responsible use of AI in publications, and adherence to ethical standards throughout the editorial process.

Target audience: The intended audience includes associate editors, reviewers, and authors, who are directly involved in managing, evaluating, and contributing scholarly manuscripts.
